text: The surgeon visually inspects the liver and gallbladder, confirming no evidence of carcinomatosis. The terminal ileum is positioned to enter the pelvis. The duodenum and pancreas are identified, along with the middle colic vessels and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The iliacolic vessels are also noted.

The surgeon identifies the right colic artery branching from the ileocolic artery, with the appendix visible on the right side. The ureter is seen vermiculating in a thin patient. The greater omentum is lifted over the liver to expose the right colon mesentery. The ileocolic vessel is placed under tension, and a window is scored inferior to it for instrument insertion. Medial to lateral dissection is performed, carefully sweeping the retroperitoneum away from the paper-thin mesentery.

The surgeon performs a gentle dissection around the duodenal sweep, carefully pulling up the thin tissue with one hand while sweeping down with the other, resembling a breaststroke motion. The dissection progresses underneath the colon, requiring delicate handling to avoid puncturing the fragile tissue.

The surgeon divides the ileocolic vessel using an energy device, while holding the transverse colon mesentery under tension. The duodenum is gently swept off the vessel, revealing the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and multiple branches. The transverse colon is lifted for better visualization.

The surgeon navigates through the hepatic flexure, identifying the duodenum and one of the middle colic pedicles, specifically the right branch. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg to facilitate the removal of the small bowel from the pelvis, lifting the sigmoid colon from below.

The surgeon adjusts the patient's position to reverse Trendelenburg and lifts the greater omentum off the transverse colon. The right side is lowered, and the stomach is visible, showing fusion with adjacent structures.
The surgeon manipulates the colon mesentery, pulling it down and identifying the anterior leaf of the greater omentum near the colon. The gastroepiploic vessel is visible and has been divided.

The surgical team adjusts the patient to a Trendelenburg position with the left side elevated. The surgeon identifies the iliac region, noting its fused appearance, and begins dissection at the sacral promontory. The surgeon uses the left hand to apply tension and guide the cutting direction, creating a larger dissection window towards the left.

The surgeon identifies the left iliac artery and maneuvers above it, pushing it upwards. They locate the surgical plane and prepare to cut through it, ensuring to go behind the structure to divide it. The ureter is gently retracted downwards, and branches of the hypogastric nerve are visible, with careful manipulation to avoid nerve damage.

The surgeon identifies the left kidney and the tail of the pancreas, which is being pulled up due to its thinness. The pancreas is gently swept down, and the surgeon prepares to divide the remaining connected tissue.

The surgeon repositions the colon back into its anatomic position, adjusting the left side down slightly. The surgical team turns off the gas and uses a syringe to assist in the procedure, while identifying the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon requests three ALS clamps and a GIA stapler, selecting a blue cartridge for the procedure. A Babcock clamp is also requested to handle the specimen, which is prepared to be sent for further analysis.
